title: Korean Spicy Gochujang Noodles

subs.name: thin rice noodles (soaked in hot water for 10 minutes) for a gluten-free adaptation
2 tbsp sriracha or sambal oelek mixed with 1 tsp soy sauce and 1 tsp sugar
julienned raw papaya or shredded carrot — equally refreshing and crunchy

steps.text: Whisk together gochujang, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, minced garlic, grated ginger and gingelly oil in a large bowl until smooth. Taste — the sauce should be simultaneously spicy, tangy and slightly sweet. Adjust vinegar or sugar to balance.

Cook the noodles in a large pot of boiling salted water according to packet directions. Drain immediately, rinse vigorously under cold running water until completely cold, then shake off excess water. Cold noodles are essential — warm noodles absorb sauce too fast and turn gluey.
Heat 1 tsp oil in a small pan over medium heat. Add the remaining garlic and fry for 30 seconds until just golden and fragrant. Pour this garlic oil directly into the sauce bowl.
Add the cold noodles to the sauce. Toss vigorously with tongs for 1–2 minutes until every strand is evenly coated in the deep-red sauce. Add the spring onion and cucumber and toss once more.
Divide into two bowls, scatter sesame seeds on top and serve immediately — these noodles are at their best right away, before the cucumber softens.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → thin rice noodles (soaked in hot water for 10 minutes) for a gluten-free adaptation; gochujang → 2 tbsp sriracha or sambal oelek mixed with 1 tsp soy sauce and 1 tsp sugar; cucumber → julienned raw papaya or shredded carrot — equally refreshing and crunchy
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Any thin wheat noodles widely available in Indian markets — hakka noodles, seviyan, or packaged ramen-style noodles — work well here. The cold-rinsed noodle technique is the same principle as for Indian cold pasta salads. Gochujang can be found in the import section of Indian metro supermarkets; sriracha (widely available in India) with a pinch of sugar is a ready substitute. Gingelly oil (nallennai in South Indian stores, til ka tel in the North) adds the signature Korean nuttiness.
